title: Interest rate rises push employed people and young families into mortgage stress
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 Interest rate rises push employed people and young families into mortgage stress Australian Broadcasting Corporation News business reporters Nassim Khadem and Adelaide Miller 5 May 2026 1306 words English ABCNEW (c) 2026 Australian Broadcasting Corporation More than 65,000 Australians have contacted the National Debt Helpline in financial distress since the start of the year, with most callers saying they are struggling to pay their home loans. Yesterday, the Reserve Bank lifted interest rates for a third time this year by 0.25 percentage points, taking the official cash rate from 4.10 per cent to 4.35 per cent, leaving many borrowers increasingly worried about how they will come up with the extra cash when budgets are already so tight. Bianca Gambrill's mortgage repayments have increased by more than $600 a fortnight since she bought her home during the COVID pandemic. The teacher from Newcastle in NSW said making the repayments had become increasingly challenging. "We make the minimum repayments … which means, because we didn't buy until our 40s … if we keep at this rate, we're paying off our home loan into our 70s," she told the ABC. Recently, Ms Gambrill had to take out a credit card to cover her dog's vet bills and still has not paid it off. "We went into quite significant credit card debt to do it, so I've got a $10,000 credit card debt there in order to meet those medical bills," she said. "It's very stressful, there are a lot of really hard discussions about it because all of our money is going on the mortgage." Ms Gambrill was diagnosed with ADHD as an adult and said interest rate hikes were also impacting when she could buy her medication. "This year I've had to go off meds during school holidays so I can stretch them out as they are too expensive," she said. Tens of thousands of people in 'distress' before latest rate hike Financial counsellors say that as interest rates rise, it is not just vulnerable people but many employed people and young families who are feeling the impacts. The data shows in April 2026, there were 13,933 calls to the National Debt Helpline. That compares to 11,554 callers in April 2025. The latest interest rate hike yesterday is set to tip more Australians into housing stress, financial counsellors warn. The majority of those contacting the helpline over the past few months are people facing mortgage stress — typically where more than 30 per cent of their income is being spent on paying their home loan. While mortgage stress is the main reason, other reasons people contact the helpline include credit card debt, electricity bills, debts owed to the Australian Taxation Office (ATO), and personal loans. Financial counsellors said the rising cost of fuel was also becoming a major issue, especially for people in rural and remote areas. Financial Counselling Australia CEO Domenique Meyrick said it had been the busiest start to the year. "If we compare it to last April, we've had a 21 per cent increase in calls and a 45 per cent increase in people using the chat function — that's a significant increase," she said. Ms Meyrick said recent data showed 41 per cent of people contacting National Debt Helpline were in some type of employment — 28 per cent in full-time and 14 per cent in part-time. She said 34 per cent had dependents and 27 per cent were in the 35-44 age bracket. "People in full-time employment, people in part-time employment, people with children, people from all the parts of our community are really doing it tough at the moment," she said. "For that group of people who are already in financial difficulty and have a mortgage, an interest rate hike always stings. "For some people, it [the latest rate hike] will be the straw that broke the camel's back." Other bills go unpaid As recession fears grow, Ms Meyrick said people were avoiding defaulting on their mortgages by cutting back on other essentials. "A lot of people will prioritise their housing over everything else, so they will not pay their telephone bills or their energy bills, they'll go without meals, they will skip medical appointments," Ms Meyrick said. "They do pretty much whatever they can to make sure they meet those housing costs and so by the time people have come to seek the support of a financial counsellor often … they've got no discretionary spending at all." People facing difficulty making mortgage repayments should reach out to their lenders for help, counsellors advise, as do the banks themselves. "Pick up the phone and call your bank early and say, 'Hey, I'm on the edge of financial difficulty, I need some support here', ask for hardship [relief]," she said. More than 1.6 million Australians 'at risk' of mortgage stress Separate data released in late April from Roy Morgan showed that with the RBA raising the cash rate to 4.35 per cent, the share of borrowers "at risk" of mortgage stress is forecast to rise to 30.4 per cent, equivalent to 1.64 million people. If the RBA raises rates again in June by 0.25 percentage points to 4.6 per cent, the share of borrowers at risk would increase to 30.9 per cent, equivalent to 1.67 million people, up 219,000 from before the May and June rate hikes. Roy Morgan's data considers borrowers at risk if they paying more than a certain proportion of their after-tax household income (25 per cent to 45 per cent depending on income and spending) into their home loan. Roy Morgan chief executive Michele Levine said it was not interest rates but unemployment that had the biggest impact on income and mortgage stress. "It's if they lose their job or main source of income," she said, adding that an ongoing war in the Middle East would increase inflation. Banks call on people in distress to get in touch early The major banks say while stressed lending and housing delinquency rates are still relatively low, more Australians are building buffers or getting in contact about repayment options before they default on their mortgages. ANZ chief financial officer Farhan Faruqi told a recent investor briefing that mortgage customers' delinquencies decreased three basis points in the half to 83 basis points. "Our mortgage customers continue to show resilience with 88 per cent of accounts ahead on repayments, and approximately 70 per cent of customers holding savings buffers of three months or more," he said. He said the bank had not seen a material increase in customer requests for hardship relief but was "very conscious of the stress from higher interest rates and cost-of-living pressures" and was closely monitoring it. A Westpac spokeswoman said 85 per cent of customers were ahead on their mortgage repayments. Only 0.53 per cent of Westpac's total mortgage portfolio of $518 billion (excluding its RAMS business) was experiencing financial hardship and the bank was offering options for them, including deferred or reduced repayments. Of those who are in a hardship arrangement, the most common support provided was a temporary pause on repayments (66 per cent) or temporary reduced repayments (16 per cent). Property research group Cotality could not provide data on distressed home sales but said there had been a more obvious trend towards auctions being withdrawn from the market prior to going ahead. This week, the preliminary numbers showed 18.3 per cent of auctions were withdrawn without a rescheduled date. Cotality said the increase in withdrawn auctions likely represented less confidence from sellers to test the market under auction conditions, probably due to low numbers of registered bidders leading up to the auction event, or feedback from the market that vendor price expectations would not be achieved. summary: Rising interest rates in Australia have pushed over 65,000 individuals into financial distress, primarily affecting employed people and young families struggling with mortgage repayments. The Reserve Bank's recent increase to 4.35% has led to a significant rise in calls to the National Debt Helpline, with many borrowers prioritising housing costs over other essential expenses. Forecasts indicate that nearly 1.7 million Australians could be at risk of mortgage stress if rates continue to rise.
publication:
Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think
theaustralian.com.au04 May 2026 20:30 GMT783 wordsJared Lynch

Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Interest rates have increased to 4.35%, causing mortgage stress for many Australians.
Over 65,000 people have contacted the National Debt Helpline this year, with a 21% increase in calls compared to last year.
Approximately 1.64 million Australians are projected to be at risk of mortgage stress due to rising rates.
relevance: Understanding the impact of interest rate changes is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing risk and advising clients on financial management.
